section.title: Delight in the Law of the Lord
section.sectionRationale: The longest chapter in the Bible, this great Torah acrostic weaves twenty two stanzas, one for every letter of the Hebrew alphabet, Aleph through Taw, each of eight verses, around a single, sustained delight in God's revealed word. Standing near the end of Book V of the Psalter, it caps the wisdom psalms that praise God's law, Psalm 1 and Psalm 19, turning that praise into a whole life of prayer.
section.thesis: The psalmist's entire life, praise and obedience, affliction and tears, hope and final confession, revolves around delight in God's word, a delight that only makes full sense fulfilled in Christ, the Word made flesh, who perfectly loved and kept the law and now writes that same word on His people's hearts.
section.frameNote: Twenty two stanzas, one per Hebrew letter, each of eight verses, moving through resolve, longing, affliction, settled trust, and a final cry as a straying sheep seeking the Shepherd, with eight interwoven Hebrew words for God's word, law, testimonies, precepts, statutes, commandments, judgments, word, and promise, running through nearly every verse.

units.label: Aleph
units.note: the blessed way of blameless obedience

verses.text: Blessed are those whose way is blameless, who walk in the {{torah|Law}} of the LORD.
Blessed are those who keep His {{edut|testimonies}} and seek Him with all their heart.
They do no iniquity; they walk in His ways.
You have ordained Your {{piqqudim|precepts}}, that we should keep them diligently.
Oh, that my ways were committed to keeping Your {{chuqqim|statutes}}!
Then I would not be ashamed when I consider all Your {{mitzvot|commandments}}.
I will praise You with an upright heart when I learn Your righteous {{mishpatim|judgments}}.
I will keep Your statutes; do not utterly forsake me.

units.label: Beth
units.note: guarding a pure way by treasuring God's word

verses.text: How can a young man keep his way pure? By guarding it according to Your {{dabar|word}}.
With all my heart I have sought You; do not let me stray from Your commandments.
I have hidden Your word in my heart that I might not sin against You.
Blessed are You, O LORD; teach me Your statutes.
With my lips I proclaim all the judgments of Your mouth.
I rejoice in the way of Your testimonies as much as in all riches.
I will meditate on Your precepts and regard Your ways.
I will delight in Your statutes; I will not forget Your word.

units.label: Gimel
units.note: a stranger longing to see wondrous things in the law

verses.text: Deal bountifully with Your servant, that I may live and keep Your word.
Open my eyes that I may see wondrous things from Your law.
I am a stranger on the earth; do not hide Your commandments from me.
My soul is consumed with longing for Your judgments at all times.
You rebuke the arrogant, the cursed who stray from Your commandments.
Remove my scorn and contempt, for I have kept Your testimonies.
Though rulers sit and slander me, Your servant meditates on Your statutes.
Your testimonies are indeed my delight; they are my counselors.

units.label: Daleth
units.note: revival from the dust through God's word

verses.text: My soul {{davaq|cleaves}} to the dust; revive me according to Your word.
I recounted my ways, and You answered me; teach me Your statutes.
Make clear to me the way of Your precepts; then I will meditate on Your wonders.
My soul melts with sorrow; strengthen me according to Your word.
Remove me from the path of deceit and graciously grant me Your law.
I have chosen the way of truth; I have set Your ordinances before me.
I {{davaq|cling}} to Your testimonies, O LORD; let me not be put to shame.
I run in the path of Your commandments, for You will enlarge my heart.

units.label: He
units.note: praying for a heart taught and turned toward God's statutes

verses.text: Teach me, O LORD, the way of Your statutes, and I will keep them to the end.
Give me understanding that I may obey Your law, and follow it with all my heart.
Direct me in the path of Your commandments, for there I find delight.
Turn my heart to Your testimonies and not to covetous gain.
Turn my eyes away from worthless things; revive me with Your word.
Establish Your word to Your servant, to produce reverence for You.
Turn away the disgrace I dread, for Your judgments are good.
How I long for Your precepts! Revive me in Your righteousness.

units.label: Waw
units.note: bold testimony and freedom found in the commandments

verses.text: May Your loving devotion come to me, O LORD, Your salvation, according to Your {{imrah|promise}}.
Then I can answer him who taunts, for I trust in Your word.
Never take Your word of truth from my mouth, for I hope in Your judgments.
I will always obey Your law, forever and ever.
And I will walk in freedom, for I have sought Your precepts.
I will speak of Your testimonies before kings, and I will not be ashamed.
I delight in Your commandments because I love them.
I lift up my hands to Your commandments, which I love, and I meditate on Your statutes.

units.label: Zayin
units.note: comfort and song amid affliction and scorn

verses.text: Remember Your word to Your servant, upon which You have given me hope.
This is my comfort in affliction, that Your promise has given me life.
The arrogant utterly deride me, but I do not turn from Your law.
I remember Your judgments of old, O LORD, and in them I find comfort.
Rage has taken hold of me because of the wicked who reject Your law.
Your statutes are songs to me in the house of my pilgrimage.
In the night, O LORD, I remember Your name, that I may keep Your law.
This is my practice, for I obey Your precepts.

units.label: Heth
units.note: the LORD as portion, sought with the whole heart

verses.text: The LORD is my portion; I have promised to keep Your words.
I have sought Your face with all my heart; be gracious to me according to Your promise.
I considered my ways and turned my steps to Your testimonies.
I hurried without hesitating to keep Your commandments.
Though the ropes of the wicked bind me, I do not forget Your law.
At midnight I rise to give You thanks for Your righteous judgments.
I am a friend to all who fear You, and to those who keep Your precepts.
The earth is filled with Your loving devotion, O LORD; teach me Your statutes.

units.label: Teth
units.note: affliction that teaches, and the law more precious than gold

verses.text: You are good to Your servant, O LORD, according to Your word.
Teach me good judgment and knowledge, for I believe in Your commandments.
Before I was afflicted, I went astray; but now I keep Your word.
You are good, and You do what is good; teach me Your statutes.
Though the arrogant have smeared me with lies, I keep Your precepts with all my heart.
Their hearts are callous and insensitive, but I delight in Your law.
It was good for me to be afflicted, that I might learn Your statutes.
The law from Your mouth is more precious to me than thousands of pieces of gold and silver.

units.label: Yodh
units.note: made by God's hands, refined through righteous affliction

verses.text: Your hands have made me and fashioned me; give me understanding to learn Your commandments.
May those who fear You see me and rejoice, for I have hoped in Your word.
I know, O LORD, that Your judgments are righteous, and that in faithfulness You have afflicted me.
May Your loving devotion comfort me, I pray, according to Your promise to Your servant.
May Your compassion come to me, that I may live, for Your law is my delight.
May the arrogant be put to shame for subverting me with a lie; I will meditate on Your precepts.
May those who fear You turn to me, those who know Your testimonies.
May my heart be blameless in Your statutes, that I may not be put to shame.

units.label: Kaph
units.note: a fainting soul waiting for God's word amid persecution

verses.text: My soul faints for Your salvation; I wait for Your word.
My eyes fail, looking for Your promise; I ask, "When will You comfort me?"
Though I am like a wineskin dried up by smoke, I do not forget Your statutes.
How many days must Your servant wait? When will You execute judgment on my persecutors?
The arrogant have dug pits for me in violation of Your law.
All Your commandments are faithful; I am persecuted without cause, help me!
They almost wiped me from the earth, but I have not forsaken Your precepts.
Revive me according to Your loving devotion, that I may obey the testimony of Your mouth.

units.label: Lamedh
units.note: God's word settled forever in heaven and earth

verses.text: Your word, O LORD, is everlasting; it is firmly fixed in the heavens.
Your faithfulness continues through all generations; You established the earth, and it endures.
Your ordinances stand to this day, for all things are servants to You.
If Your law had not been my delight, then I would have perished in my affliction.
I will never forget Your precepts, for by them You have revived me.
I am Yours; save me, for I have sought Your precepts.
The wicked wait to destroy me, but I will ponder Your testimonies.
I have seen a limit to all perfection, but Your commandment is without limit.

units.label: Mem
units.note: wisdom and sweetness surpassing teachers and honey

verses.text: Oh, how I love Your {{torah|law}}! All day long it is my meditation.
Your commandments make me wiser than my enemies, for they are always with me.
I have more insight than all my teachers, for Your testimonies are my meditation.
I discern more than the elders, for I obey Your precepts.
I have kept my feet from every evil path, that I may keep Your word.
I have not departed from Your ordinances, for You Yourself have taught me.
How sweet are Your words to my taste, sweeter than honey in my mouth!
I gain understanding from Your precepts; therefore I hate every false way.

units.label: Nun
units.note: the word as lamp and light for the way

verses.text: Your {{dabar|word}} is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path.
I have sworn and confirmed that I will keep Your righteous judgments.
I am severely afflicted, O LORD; revive me through Your word.
Accept the freewill offerings of my mouth, O LORD, and teach me Your judgments.
I constantly take my life in my hands, yet I do not forget Your law.
The wicked have set a snare for me, but I have not strayed from Your precepts.
Your {{edut|testimonies}} are my heritage forever, for they are the joy of my heart.
I have inclined my heart to perform Your statutes, even to the very end.

units.label: Samekh
units.note: hatred of double mindedness, hiding in God alone

verses.text: The double-minded I despise, but Your law I love.
You are my hiding place and my shield; I put my hope in Your word.
Depart from me, you evildoers, that I may obey the commandments of my God.
Sustain me as You promised, that I may live; let me not be ashamed of my hope.
Uphold me, and I will be saved, that I may always regard Your statutes.
You reject all who stray from Your statutes, for their deceitfulness is in vain.
All the wicked on earth You discard like dross; therefore I love Your testimonies.
My flesh trembles in awe of You; I stand in fear of Your judgments.

units.label: Ayin
units.note: pleading for justice and understanding as God's servant

verses.text: I have done what is just and right; do not leave me to my oppressors.
Ensure Your servant's well-being; do not let the arrogant oppress me.
My eyes fail, looking for Your salvation, and for Your righteous promise.
Deal with Your servant according to Your loving devotion, and teach me Your statutes.
I am Your servant; give me understanding, that I may know Your testimonies.
It is time for the LORD to act, for they have broken Your law.
Therefore I love Your {{mitzvot|commandments}} more than gold, even the purest gold.
Therefore I admire all Your precepts and hate every false way.

units.label: Pe
units.note: wonder at God's words, and tears over their neglect

verses.text: Wonderful are Your testimonies; therefore I obey them.
The unfolding of Your words gives light; it informs the simple.
I open my mouth and pant, longing for Your commandments.
Turn to me and show me mercy, as You do to those who love Your name.
Order my steps in Your word; let no sin rule over me.
Redeem me from the oppression of man, that I may keep Your precepts.
Make Your face shine upon Your servant, and teach me Your statutes.
My eyes shed streams of tears because Your law is not obeyed.

units.label: Tzade
units.note: the everlasting righteousness of God's testimonies

verses.text: Righteous are You, O LORD, and upright are Your judgments.
The testimonies You have laid down are righteous and altogether faithful.
My zeal has consumed me because my foes forget Your words.
Your promise is completely pure; therefore Your servant loves it.
I am lowly and despised, but I do not forget Your precepts.
Your righteousness is everlasting and Your law is true.
Trouble and distress have found me, but Your commandments are my delight.
Your testimonies are righteous forever. Give me understanding, that I may live.

units.label: Koph
units.note: crying before dawn, near to the LORD

verses.text: I call with all my heart; answer me, O LORD! I will obey Your statutes.
I call to You; save me, that I may keep Your testimonies.
I rise before dawn and cry for help; in Your word I have put my hope.
My eyes anticipate the watches of night, that I may meditate on Your word.
Hear my voice, O LORD, according to Your loving devotion; give me life according to Your justice.
Those who follow after wickedness draw near; they are far from Your law.
You are near, O LORD, and all Your commandments are true.
Long ago I learned from Your testimonies that You have established them forever.

units.label: Resh
units.note: pleading for revival, resting in the sum of God's word

verses.text: Look upon my affliction and rescue me, for I have not forgotten Your law.
Defend my cause and redeem me; revive me according to Your word.
Salvation is far from the wicked because they do not seek Your statutes.
Great are Your mercies, O LORD; revive me according to Your ordinances.
Though my persecutors and foes are many, I have not turned from Your testimonies.
I look on the faithless with loathing because they do not keep Your word.
Consider how I love Your precepts, O LORD; give me life according to Your loving devotion.
The entirety of Your {{dabar|word}} is truth, and all Your righteous judgments endure forever.

units.label: Sin and Shin
units.note: great peace and joyful obedience despite persecution

verses.text: Rulers persecute me without cause, but my heart fears only Your word.
I rejoice in Your promise like one who finds great spoil.
I hate and abhor falsehood, but Your law I love.
Seven times a day I praise You for Your righteous judgments.
Abundant peace belongs to those who love Your law; nothing can make them stumble.
I wait for Your salvation, O LORD, and I carry out Your commandments.
I obey Your testimonies and love them greatly.
I obey Your precepts and Your testimonies, for all my ways are before You.

units.label: Taw
units.note: a final cry and confession as a lost sheep seeking the Shepherd

verses.text: May my cry come before You, O LORD; give me understanding according to Your word.
May my plea come before You; rescue me according to Your promise.
My lips pour forth praise, for You teach me Your statutes.
My tongue sings of Your word, for all Your commandments are righteous.
May Your hand be ready to help me, for I have chosen Your precepts.
I long for Your salvation, O LORD, and Your law is my delight.
Let me live to praise You; may Your judgments sustain me.
I have strayed like a lost sheep; seek Your servant, for I have not forgotten Your {{mitzvot|commandments}}.

torah.gloss: Instruction, teaching, law. The psalm's master term, appearing about twenty five times, torah means far more than a list of rules, it is the whole of God's revealed teaching for life, treasured here as the psalmist's greatest delight.

edut.gloss: Testimonies, the covenant stipulations that testify to who God is and what He requires, recalling the tablets of testimony given at Sinai and carried in the ark.

piqqudim.gloss: Precepts, charges entrusted to someone for careful keeping, from a root meaning to appoint or oversee, God's precepts are His personal charge over the psalmist's life.

chuqqim.gloss: Statutes, decrees inscribed or engraved as fixed boundaries, the same word used for the enduring boundaries God sets over creation and covenant alike.

mitzvot.gloss: Commandments, direct and authoritative commands, the most straightforward Hebrew term for what God has ordered His people to do.

mishpatim.gloss: Judgments or rules, God's just verdicts and case decisions, the term underscores that His commands are not arbitrary but flow from perfect justice.

dabar.gloss: Word, the broadest term for what God has spoken, His word in general, whether commanding, promising, or sustaining, the same word later applied to the eternal Word made flesh, John 1:1, 14.

imrah.gloss: Word or promise, an intimate, poetic synonym for dabar, often rendered promise, it emphasizes God's spoken assurance as personal and trustworthy.

davaq.gloss: To cling, cleave, stay close. The psalmist uses the same root for his soul clinging to the dust in discouragement, verse 25, and clinging to God's testimonies in devotion, verse 31, showing the whole self is drawn either downward or toward God.

basic.context: Psalm 119 is the longest chapter in the Bible, an alphabetic acrostic of twenty two stanzas, one for each letter of the Hebrew alphabet, Aleph to Taw, each built from eight verses that, in the Hebrew, begin with that letter. It stands near the end of Book V of the Psalter, capping the wisdom psalms that praise God's law, compare Psalm 1 and Psalm 19, and it works as a sustained love song to God's word, using eight near synonyms, law, testimonies, precepts, statutes, commandments, judgments, word, and promise, woven through nearly every verse.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalmist is not a legalist trying to earn God's favor, he already stands within God's covenant love and delights in His word because he loves God, not to purchase that love. Read alongside the New Testament, this delight is not opposed to grace, it is what grace produces in a heart that trusts God. The psalm mixes resolve, lament, and praise, and its many prayers, teach me, revive me, turn my heart, show that even love for the law depends on God's own enabling work.
basic.originalLanguages: Eight Hebrew words for God's revealed will recur throughout, torah, law or instruction, edut, testimonies, piqqudim, precepts, chuqqim, statutes, mitzvot, commandments, mishpatim, judgments, and dabar and imrah, word and promise. The variety is not decoration, it shows that every angle of God's revealed word, whether commanding, instructing, witnessing, or promising, deserves the psalmist's whole hearted love.
basic.typology: The psalmist's resolve to keep God's law without fail is an ideal no ordinary reader fully reaches, and so it points forward to the one who did keep the law perfectly and loved it entirely, Jesus Christ. His many prayers to be taught, revived, and upheld anticipate the greater grace of the new covenant, when God writes His law not on stone but on the heart.
basic.christ: This psalm's love for God's spoken word finds its fulfillment in Jesus, the Word made flesh, John 1:1, 14. Jesus said He came not to abolish the Law and the Prophets but to fulfill them, Matthew 5:17, and He alone lived out this psalm's ideal without failure. His own testimony, I always do what pleases Him, John 8:29, answers the psalmist's many prayers for a heart that truly keeps God's statutes. And what the psalmist could only ask God to do, write the word on the heart, God promises through the new covenant Christ inaugurates, Jeremiah 31:33, 2 Corinthians 3:3.
basic.god: God is the giver of a good and trustworthy word, righteous in all His judgments, verse 137, and near to all who call on Him, verse 151. He afflicts in faithfulness, not cruelty, verse 75, using hardship to teach His statutes, and He is the one the psalmist depends on for every step of obedience, teach me, revive me, uphold me, turn my heart, so that even delight in the law is finally a gift of grace.
basic.crossReferences: John 1:1-14 identifies Jesus as the eternal Word made flesh. Matthew 5:17-18 shows Jesus fulfilling, not abolishing, the law. Jeremiah 31:31-34 and 2 Corinthians 3:3 promise God's law written on the heart by His Spirit. Psalm 19:7-11 is this psalm's shorter companion in praising God's perfect law.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's sustained delight in God's word belongs to the story that runs from Sinai's law, through Israel's repeated failure to keep it, to the promise of a new covenant heart, and finds its center in Christ, the Word made flesh, who kept the law perfectly, bore its curse, and now writes that same law on the hearts of His people by His Spirit.

context.literary: An alphabetic acrostic of twenty two stanzas of eight verses each, Aleph to Taw, one of several acrostic psalms, compare Psalms 25, 34, 37, 111, 112, 145, but by far the longest and most elaborate, built almost entirely from eight interwoven synonyms for God's revealed word.
context.historical: Likely postexilic in setting, close in feel to Ezra and Nehemiah's zeal for the law, Nehemiah 8, though the text names no author or occasion. Its language of persecution, exile like alienation, a stranger on the earth, verse 19, and longing for vindication fits a community pressed by hostile rulers yet clinging to Torah as its identity and hope.
context.canonical: Sits within Book V of the Psalter alongside the Songs of Ascents, forming a climactic meditation on the law that echoes Psalm 1's opening blessing on the one who delights in the LORD's instruction and Psalm 19's praise of the perfect, soul reviving law, gathering both into one extended, personal devotion.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach God's people, through sustained meditation and prayer, to treasure His revealed word above all else, and to show that such delight is sustained not by human willpower but by God's own teaching, reviving, and upholding grace.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm prescribes an abiding pattern, wholehearted delight in and obedience to God's word, sustained by prayer for God's own help, rather than describing a single historical event. Its specific circumstances, persecution by rulers, affliction, night watches, are illustrative rather than mandatory patterns to repeat.

debates.question: Does the psalmist's love for 'the law' describe devotion to the Mosaic law code specifically, or to God's revealed word in the fuller sense, including promise?

views.label: Primarily the Mosaic law, torah, as covenant instruction
views.case: The Hebrew torah most often refers to the instruction given at Sinai, and the psalm's vocabulary, statutes, commandments, judgments, closely tracks covenant law terminology used throughout the Pentateuch.

views.label: God's whole revealed word, law and promise together
views.case: The psalm also uses dabar and imrah, word and promise, and repeatedly grounds hope in God's promises rather than merely His commands, so torah functions more broadly as the whole of God's revealed instruction, encompassing gospel promise as well as command.

debates.bearing: Either way, the New Testament reveals that the law itself pointed forward to Christ, Galatians 3:24, so the psalmist's love for Torah, read in full canonical light, is love for the very word that promised, and has now produced, the Savior.

study.originalLanguages: Torah, תּוֹרָה, instruction or law, is the psalm's master term, appearing about twenty five times, but seven companion words keep the vocabulary from becoming repetitive: edut, עֵדוּת, testimonies, the covenant stipulations that testify to God's character, piqqudim, פִּקּוּדִים, precepts, charges entrusted for careful keeping, chuqqim, חֻקִּים, statutes, decrees inscribed as fixed boundaries, mitzvot, מִצְוֹת, commandments, direct authoritative commands, mishpatim, מִשְׁפָּטִים, judgments or rules, God's just verdicts, and dabar, דָּבָר, and imrah, אִמְרָה, word and promise, the broadest terms for what God has spoken. The psalmist also uses davaq, דָּבַק, to cling, both negatively, his soul cleaves to the dust, verse 25, and positively, he clings to God's testimonies, verse 31, showing that the whole self, not merely the mind, is drawn either downward or toward God.

typology.type: The psalmist's flawless resolve to keep God's law, verse 44, I will always obey Your law, forever and ever
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled only in Christ, who alone perfectly kept the law and could say, I always do what pleases Him, John 8:29.

typology.type: The plea to have God's word planted in the heart, verse 11, I have hidden Your word in my heart
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the new covenant promise that God will write His law on His people's hearts by His Spirit, Jeremiah 31:33, 2 Corinthians 3:3.

typology.type: The word as lamp and light for the path, verse 105
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the light of the world, John 8:12, the Word made flesh who is Himself the light that guides, John 1:1-9.

study.christ: This psalm's central confession, love for God's word in all its forms, law, testimonies, precepts, statutes, commandments, judgments, word, and promise, finds its fulfillment in Jesus, the Word made flesh, John 1:1, 14, who declared He came not to abolish the Law and the Prophets but to fulfill them, Matthew 5:17. Where the psalmist can only long and pray to keep God's statutes without fail, Christ actually lived that life, saying, I always do what pleases Him, John 8:29. And where the psalmist prays, teach me, revive me, turn my heart, the new covenant Christ establishes answers that prayer directly, God's law written not on tablets of stone but on hearts of flesh, Jeremiah 31:33, 2 Corinthians 3:3.
study.god: God is shown as the giver of a trustworthy, righteous, and everlasting word, verse 89, near to all who call on Him, verse 151, and active even in affliction, using hardship in faithfulness to teach His people, verse 75. Far from a distant lawgiver, He is the one the psalmist depends on for every step, to teach, revive, uphold, and turn the heart, so that delight in His law is finally His own gift, not merely human effort.

crossReferences.note: The Word, Logos, that was with God and was God becomes flesh, the ultimate fulfillment of the psalmist's love for God's spoken word.
Jesus declares He came to fulfill, not abolish, the Law and the Prophets, the perfect keeper this psalm's ideal anticipates.
Promises a new covenant in which God's law is written on the heart, the fulfillment of the psalmist's repeated prayer, teach me, turn my heart.
Believers are a letter of Christ, written not with ink but with the Spirit, on hearts of flesh, echoing this psalm's hope for inward, Spirit given obedience.
A shorter companion psalm praising the perfection of God's law, reviving the soul and giving joy to the heart, the same themes Psalm 119 develops at length.
The word of God is living and active, echoing this psalm's confidence that God's word gives life and searches the heart.

study.oneStory: This psalm's sustained delight in God's word belongs to the great story that runs from the giving of the law at Sinai, through Israel's repeated failure to keep it, to the prophetic promise of a new covenant heart, and finds its center in Christ, the Word made flesh, who kept the law perfectly, bore its curse in His death, and now writes that same word on the hearts of His people by His Spirit, so that what the psalmist could only long for, unbroken delight and obedience, becomes a gift given in Him.

sermon.title: An Exposition of Psalm 119, Part One
sermon.why: Begg works through Psalm 119:97 to 112 to show that the Bible comes from God not only to instruct but to communicate His love, and that meditating on God's word is how the Spirit enables obedience and conforms believers to Christ, directly serving this study's theme of delight in the law of the LORD.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist mean by loving God's law throughout this psalm?

options.text: A grateful, wholehearted delight in God's revealed instruction, sustained by His own grace, not a means of earning His favor.
options.note: The psalm's constant prayers for God's help show delight in the law flows from grace, not toward earning it.

options.text: A strict, joyless rule keeping meant to earn God's approval.
options.note: This mistakes law keeping for legalism; the psalm's tone throughout is one of delight, not anxious earning.

options.text: A poetic exaggeration with no real bearing on daily obedience.
options.note: The psalm is intensely practical, shaping daily choices, speech, and worship.

options.text: A rejection of God's grace in favor of self-effort.
options.note: The psalmist constantly prays for God's help, teach me, revive me, showing dependence on grace, not self-effort.

quiz.q: How many stanzas structure Psalm 119, and on what pattern?

options.text: Twenty two stanzas of eight verses each, one for every letter of the Hebrew alphabet, Aleph to Taw.
options.note: This acrostic structure is the psalm's defining literary feature.

options.text: Twelve stanzas of varying length grouped loosely by theme.
options.note: The psalm's stanzas are fixed at eight verses each, tied to the Hebrew alphabet, not loose thematic groupings.

options.text: Seven stanzas, one for each day of creation.
options.note: There is no connection here to the creation week; the structure follows the Hebrew alphabet.

options.text: There is no discernible structure; the verses are randomly arranged.
options.note: The opposite is true, the psalm is one of the most tightly ordered acrostics in the Bible, though its careful design is easy to miss without knowing Hebrew.

quiz.q: According to verse 105, what is God's word compared to?

options.text: A lamp to my feet and a light to my path.
options.note: This is one of the psalm's most famous images, God's word as guidance for every step.

options.text: A sword that divides soul and spirit.
options.note: That vivid image belongs to Hebrews 4:12, not this verse.

options.text: A shield that stops every arrow.
options.note: Shield imagery appears elsewhere in the psalm, verse 114, but verse 105 specifically names a lamp and a light.

options.text: A silent, distant voice.
options.note: The opposite is true; the word is near and active, guiding every step of the way.

quiz.q: How does the psalm's picture of loving God's law point forward to Christ?

options.text: Christ alone perfectly kept and fulfilled the law, and He is the Word made flesh who now writes that same word on believers' hearts by His Spirit.
options.note: This connects the psalm directly to John 1:1-14, Matthew 5:17, and Jeremiah 31:33.

options.text: It shows only that Old Testament believers related to God differently than New Testament believers do.
options.note: There is a redemptive-historical development, but the deeper point is direct fulfillment in Christ, not a sharp discontinuity.

options.text: It has no connection with the New Testament at all.
options.note: The New Testament draws on this psalm's themes repeatedly, from the Word made flesh to the law written on the heart.

options.text: It proves salvation was earned by law keeping in the Old Testament and only by grace in the New.
options.note: This misreads both testaments; grace sustains the psalmist's obedience throughout, just as it does for believers after Christ.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist's affliction, for example verse 71, teach about suffering?

options.text: God can use hardship, in faithfulness, to teach His people to treasure His word more deeply.
options.note: Verse 71 explicitly says affliction was good because it taught the psalmist God's statutes.

options.text: Suffering always means God is angry and punishing sin.
options.note: This overstates the point; the text frames affliction as God's faithful discipline for growth, not merely punitive anger.

options.text: Suffering is meaningless and should be avoided at all costs.
options.note: This contradicts the psalmist's own testimony that affliction taught him God's statutes.

options.text: Suffering proves God has abandoned His people.
options.note: The opposite is true; the psalmist experiences God as near precisely amid affliction, verse 151.

questions: The psalmist prays teach me, revive me, turn my heart, again and again. Where in your life do you need to ask God, rather than relying on your own willpower, to produce delight in His word?

Eight different words for God's word run through this psalm, law, testimonies, precepts, statutes, commandments, judgments, word, promise. Which of these facets of God speaking, commanding, teaching, promising, judging, is hardest for you to receive with delight rather than resistance?

The psalmist calls himself a stranger on the earth in verse 19 and confesses he has strayed like a lost sheep in verse 176, in the very same psalm. How do both being a devoted stranger and a straying sheep describe your own walk with God right now?
Verse 71 says affliction taught the psalmist God's statutes. What hardship in your life might God be using to deepen your love for His word, rather than to punish you?

Christ is the Word made flesh who perfectly loved and fulfilled the law the psalmist only longed to keep. How does resting in His finished obedience change the way you approach your own obedience this week?

translationNotes.note: Law renders torah, broader than a list of rules, it means instruction or teaching, the whole of God's revealed will for His people.
The BSB's original em dashes in these verses have been rendered here as commas for house style; no wording has been altered.
Callous and insensitive can also be rendered fat, a Hebrew idiom for a heart dulled toward God by ease and self-satisfaction.
This stanza heading combines two related Hebrew letters, Sin and Shin, which share one written form distinguished only by a dot, the only stanza in the psalm built on a doubled letter.
